coredata.dc:title: Doxorubicin loaded carboxymethyl cellulose/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel films as a potential anticancer drug delivery system
coredata.dc:creator: Javanbakht, Siamak
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Creating anticancer properties in the hydrogel film could make it as a candidate for treating cancer tissues. In this work, a novel hydrogel nanocomposite films with anticancer properties were designed via incorporation of graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a nanoparticle into carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hydrogel and using doxorubicin (DOX) as drug model with broad-spectrum anticancer properties. Drug release studies carried out at two different pHs and the MTT assay was evaluated for DOX-loaded CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films against blood cancer cells (K562). The prepared nanocomposite hydrogel films were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–Vis spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), permeability and mechanical properties. The prepared CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films showed an improvement in vitro swelling, degradation, water vapor permeability and pH-sensitive drug delivery properties along with not significant toxicity against blood cancer cells (K562). According to the obtained results, this nanocomposite hydrogel films can be proposed to use as an anticancer film and drug delivery system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite hydrogel
Graphene quantum dot
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films
Thickness and tensile properties of the films
GQD-dependent in vitro drug release and load
Morphological study of the apoptotic cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQD: SEM (a), DLS spectrum (a′), Photoluminescence spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs and the photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs under 365 nm UV light (b), the photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of a powder and film of CMC/GQDs nanocomposite under 365 nm UV light (c) and XRD pattern of GQD, pure DOX, and DOX-GQD (d).
FT-IR spectra for the GQD, pure CMC, and CMC/GQD nanocomposites.
SEM images of the CMC (a) and CMC/GQD nanocomposites containing, 10% (b), 20% (c) and 30% GQDs (d).
Tensile strength (a) and Elongation at break (b) for the pure CMC and CMC/GQD nanocomposite films with different GQDs concentration (n = 3, *P < 0.05).

(a) swelling ratio and (b) biodegradation at pH 7.4, (c) swelling ratio and (d) biodegradation at pH 4.5 for the pure CMC and CMC/GQD nanocomposite films with different GQDs concentration (n = 3, **P < 0.05).
Effect of GQDs concentration on water vapor permeability of films (n = 3, ***P < 0.05).
GQD-Dependent DOX cumulative release at pH 7.4 (a), pH 4.5 (b), and pH 7 (c) (n = 3, ****P < 0.05).

DOX loading percent of the CMC/GQD nanocomposite films with different GQDs concentration (a), schematic representation of the Π-Π stacking among the GQD nanoparticle and doxorubicin (b) (n = 3, # P < 0.05), and SEM images of the DOX/CMC (c) and DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposites containing, 10% (d), 20% (e) and 30% GQDs (f).

The pH-dependent DOX cumulative release of CMC/GQD 30% nanocomposite film (a) and cumulative release amount (mg) of DOX from CMC/GQD nanocomposite film with different GQDs concentration at pH 4.5 (b) (n = 3, ### P < 0.05).

In vitro cell viabilities of K562 cell after being incubated with various concentrations of a) CMC/GQD nanocomposite film with different GQDs concentratin and b) DOX/CMC/GQD with different GQDs concentratin and free DOX solution for 72 h (n = 3, ### P < 0.05).

Phase-contrast micrographs and fluorescence of the K562 cells treated with the DOX/CMC, DOX/CMC/GQD 10%, DOX/CMC/GQD 20% and DOX/CMC/GQD 30% (at IC50 values). (a) Phase-contrast images of the K562 cells. Control cells have a round shape indicating live cells. While, treated cells have a fragmented and condensed form. (b) Fluorescence images of the K562 cells stained with Hoechst 33258 after 72 h. Magnification, 200×.

head.ce:title: Doxorubicin loaded carboxymethyl cellulose/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel films as a potential anticancer drug delivery system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creating anticancer properties in the hydrogel film could make it as a candidate for treating cancer tissues. In this work, a novel hydrogel nanocomposite films with anticancer properties were designed via incorporation of graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a nanoparticle into carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hydrogel and using doxorubicin (DOX) as drug model with broad-spectrum anticancer properties. Drug release studies carried out at two different pHs and the MTT assay was evaluated for DOX-loaded CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films against blood cancer cells (K562). The prepared nanocomposite hydrogel films were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV–Vis spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), permeability and mechanical properties. The prepared CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films showed an improvement in vitro swelling, degradation, water vapor permeability and pH-sensitive drug delivery properties along with not significant toxicity against blood cancer cells (K562). According to the obtained results, this nanocomposite hydrogel films can be proposed to use as an anticancer film and drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past years, GQDs has been more attention, due to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects [1–3]. Enormous efforts were devoted to the GQDs synthesis (bottom-up and top-down methods) and determining their properties [4–6], while their applications in biomedical field have not been extensively studied. GQDs are appearing as undertaking materials for biomedical applications [6–9]. Due to the GQDs have high fundamental fluorescence, large surface area with delocalized electrons, unparalleled excitation relevant emission, solubility in the variety of solvents, chemical inertness, local functional groups at the edges, ability of drug loading by π–π interactions and easy variability of size and shape, have being extensively investigated for cellular imaging and drug delivery [6,7,10–12]. As known, the structures of GQDs are totally composed of “carbon” materials, and they have low cytotoxicity in comparison to inorganic quantum dots which after degradation may have toxic metal ions in their residues [7,13–15]. The importance of GQDs as a drug delivery platform could either attributed to their easy functionalization through the oxygen groups or through π–π interactions, that provide great capabilities by these characteristics [16].

Due to the hydrogels have porous and hydrated molecular structure, so these materials can stimulate the biological and physicochemical properties of tissue microenvironment [17,18]. Hydrogel networks are produced via chemical or physical crosslinking [19]. Three-dimensional hydrogel network prepared from highly hydrated polymeric can absorb environment water and swell several times under physiological conditions without dissolution [20,21]. Too many reports have involved the investigation of CMC in hydrogel usage [22–24]. Owing to the anionic and water-soluble CMC has unparalleled properties like non-toxic, biocompatibility, biodegradability, hydrophilicity, high viscosity, transparency, and good film forming ability, therefore; it has received much interest [25–28]. The pH-sensitive CMC hydrogels have the ability to absorb high water from biological environment even skin moist [29] and the potential for use in the site-specific drug delivery [18,30,31].

In chemotherapy DOX is used as a typical anticancer drug. DOX kills cells by incorporating with DNA; furthermore, it prevents the cell division and the DNA replication process [16,32]. So, it acts sufficiently in next to the cancer cells. So far, most of drug carriers such as liposomes, nanoparticles, and nancomposite are able to transfer the drug into the cellar nucleus by blood vessels; however, the offering of a system that can be directly in touch with the cancer cells by implanting of drug carrier is essential topic in this field [33]. Thus, it is urgent to prepare a drug carrier which is capable of releasing anticancer drugs effectively in the location of cancer cells and delivers the drugs into the cancer cells directly. To reach this aim, we load the DOX drug into the biodegradable hydrogel nanocomposite film which can be proposed as a carrier by planting in specified locations. GQD nanoparticles could efficiently conjunct with DOX then deliver it to the cancer tissues and dramatically enhance cytotoxicity of DOX. Furthermore, by using this strategy it is possible to increase the efficiency of chemotherapy [34]. These results expressed that the GQD-based carriers in comparison with many other common nanocarriers such as carbon nanotubes and polymeric micelles can load more DOX and also show remarkably high efficiency [16,35,36]. Thereby, GQD-based carriers are promising and highly efficient carrier for loading and delivery of DOX. Therefore, in this work, we choose DOX as the model drug for loading into the nanocomposite hydrogel films.

As part of our ongoing program, related to the developing new drug delivery system [37], based on our previous investigations on CMC [38], we have reported the biodegradable CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films as a novel anticancer drug carrier. Recently some papers were published related to the film polymer drug delivery [39,40]; however, the number of reported articles about nanocomposite hydrogel films as an anticancer drug carrier is not so much. In comparison with previous reports [41], the prepared nanocomposite hydrogel films is capable to load DOX drug with high efficiency; hence, it can be suggested as a new drug delivery system to deliver and efficiently release anticancer drugs to cancer cells. Here in, GQDs was incorporated into CMC through H-bonding and chemical reaction then CMC/GQD hydrogel films were prepared via a casting technique. The influence of GQDs content on the properties of the prepared hydrogel nanocomposites was investigated. CMC/GQD hydrogel films were used as a drug carrier and in the release process, the pH-sensitive drug release behavior was observed. The CMC/GQD hydrogel films able to start a new idea in the design of pH-triggered delivery at specific sites and anticancer film. In addition, the CMC/GQD hydrogel films can protect the drugs from degradation in the presence of different organs and tissues in the human body [42]. In vitro, the drug delivery tests and MTT assay were carried out and the effectiveness of this novel nanocomposite system was verified. It seems that this carrier system could be potential to use in the anticancer film and drug delivery systems.

Sodium CMC, the degree of substitution (DS) 0.55–1.0, and viscosity 15,000 MP/s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) were obtained from Nippon Paper Chemicals Co., Ltd., Japan. DOX was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Citric acid (CA), glycerol, epichlorohydrin (ECH) and all other materials were purchased from Merck.

UV–Vis absorption spectra and Infrared spectra of the samples were obtained on a Shimadzu 1700 Model UV–vis spectrophotometer and FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) in the 4000–400 cm−1 range at a resolution of 0.5 cm−1 as KBr pellets, respectively. The samples surface morphology was investigated using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (LEO 1430VP) operated at 15 kV after coating the samples with gold films. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) was conducted by LEO 906E transmission electron microscope operating at 100 kV. The photoluminescence spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs were recorded by a spectrofluorometric (LS45, PerkinElmer). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were obtained with a DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed at room temperature by Siemens diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV in the scan range of 2θ from 5 to 70°. X'Pert High Score Plus was used to refine the single-crystal parameters to the measured powder diffraction profile.

The GQDs was prepared in accordance with previous reports from direct pyrolysis of CA [43]. In a typical procedure, 2 g CA was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle. About 30 min later, the obtained orange liquid was added drop by drop into 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized with NaOH, the 2% aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: First, 48 mL of an aqueous solution of the different amount of GQDs were prepared by adding 6 mL, 12 mL and 18 mL for 10%, 20%, and 30% GQDs content, respectively. 1.2 g of CMC was added to the resulted solution with a continuous mechanical stirring to reach a homogeneous viscous mixture. Then Glycerol as a plasticizer (0.6 g) and ECH as a cross linker (2 mL) were added to this solution. At the end, 30 g of formed paste was cast to a 9 cm diameter polystyrene plate and dried at 60 °C an oven at 12 h. The average thickness of the films was 10 μm.

The analysis of degradation and swelling were measured by immersing a some weighed sample with known size in 10 mL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and sodium acetate buffered (SAB) at physiological temperature and different pH. At predetermined time intervals, the samples were taken out and blotted onto filter paper to remove surface water. To determine degradation, the film was then dried at 25 °C in the oven for 24 h. The experiment was carried out in triplicates. The swelling ratio (Eq. (1)) and degradation (Eq. (2)) were defined as:
(1)
Swelling ratio =
Wt − Wi
Wi
where Wt = wet weight of the sample at time t and Wi = initial dry weight.
(2)
Weight loss =
Wi − Wt
d
Wi
× 100 %
where Wt(d) = dry weight of sample collected at time t.

Gravimetrically method was used to determine the water vapor permeability (WVP) of films (g m−1h−1Pa−1) at 20 °C [37,44]. The opening head of glass vessels with 2.5 diameters, containing 15 mL distilled water sealed off with the prepared nanocomposite films having silicone grease on. Desiccators containing silicagel used to obtain an RH gradient equal to 100%. The vessels were located in desiccators at 20 °C and weight loss was measured as a function of time, which is in occurrence with the water vapor transfers through the wide-open film area (4.9 cm2). WVP was computed from the Eq. (3). When steady-state conditions were achieved (about 2 h), eight weight measurements were read each hour using a four-digit balance. This test was repeated at least three times for each sample.
(3)
WVP =
S × d
A × ∆P

Where S is the slope of the weight loss vs. time (g h−1), d is the film thickness (m), A is the area of exposed film (m2) and ΔP is the differential of water vapor pressure across the film (at 20 °C, ΔP = 2.33 × 103 Pa, assuming that the RH on the silicagel is negligible).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness and tensile properties of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the film samples was measured by SEM. According to the standard test method of ASTM D 882-88 the elongation at break and tensile strength of the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films were measured using Instron Universal Testing Machine (SANTAM, Model STM1, Iran).

For the loading DOX in the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films, same mass (400 mg) from each sample was swelled in 40 mL from 100 ppm DOX concentration. After a continuous shaking for 72 h in the dark, the DOX loading capacity determined using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 483 nm. DOX-loaded CMC/GQD (DOX/CMC/GQD) washed thoroughly to remove unloaded drug and dry at 25 °C in the oven.

To study the pH-sensitivity release of drugs, 10 mg drug-loaded hydrogels were immersed in 10 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) and SAB (pH 4.5). Then at appropriate time intervals, 3 mL of aqueous solution containing drug was withdrawn and replaced with aliquots fresh buffer. The capacity load and cumulative release data were obtained by using a predetermined calibration curve for each drug. The amounts of drug loaded and released were calculated by the following two formulas (Eqs. (4) and (5)):
(4)
Drug loading capacity =
weight of drug in the substrate
w eight of the substrate
× 100
(5)
Drug release =
the amount of released drug the amount of loaded drug
× 100

MTT assay was evaluated for the in vitro cell toxicity of DOX-loaded CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films. According to the typical procedure [45], K562 cells (human blood cancer cell lines) were selected. In summary, K562 cells were seeded on 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well in 100 μL cell medium and incubated overnight. Then the resultant were treated with free DOX and DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite with an equal concentration of DOX at 32, 16, 8, 4 and 2 μg/mL. Also in order to consider its biocompatibility, the cells were treated with blank nano-carrier. With using the MTT method after incubation for 72 h, cell toxicity was assessed. 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added after removing the culture medium of each well. The culture medium was removed after 4 h incubation at 37 °C then, 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. By manual counting with a haemacytometer, the cells' number were determined. For every type of hydrogel films, the culturing experiments were repeated in triplicate. Inverted microscope (Zeiss) was used for the evaluation of the cell morphology. Fluorescence microscope (Zeiss Axoscope 2 Plus) equipped with appropriate filter sets DNA was stained with Hoechst 33258 (1 mg/mL) in PBS for 5 min, and then washed twice with PBS for 5 min each, then examined.

SEM image of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 1a. This image illustrated uniform circular shape with a narrow range distribution of particle size from 4 to 6 nm which was confirmed by DLS experiments (Fig. 1a′). Fig.1b exhibited the PL and UV–Vis spectrum and a digital photograph of GQDs solution under UV lamp (365 nm). A wide (002) peak around 2θ 26.98° in the XRD patterns of GQDs was detected (Fig. 1d), that is good evidence for carbonizing of CA to yield graphene structures [46]. These characterizing of GQD that is shown in Fig. 1 individually is similar to previous reports for the GQD synthesized from CA [43]. The XRD data revealed the crystalline nature of the drug and compatibility between drug and GQD [47,48]. As well as photoluminescent properties were created in the nanocomposite hydrogel films by incorporation of GQDs nanoparticle in the polymeric matrix. Fig.1c shows the photoluminescent properties of the composite hydrogel films.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of CMC, GQDs and CMC/GQD hydrogel nanocomposite films. In the GQDs spectrum, the absorption of stretching vibration of CH in the range lower 1350 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH at 2950 cm−1 was detected. Indeed, the skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1 were detected. In the CMC spectrum, the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group was observed at the 3375 cm−1 and the peak at 2930 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of –CH2. The spectrum of the CMC/GQD was similar to the combining bands of GQD and CMC, which could be a good evidence for interactions between nanoparticles and the polymeric matrix.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the pure CMC hydrogel and CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films. The pure CMC hydrogel exhibited a uniform surface morphology. Moreover, with an increase of GQDs nanoparticle concentration, the surface morphology of the film was changed. However, there are visible well dispersion nanoparticles in the surface of nanocomposite hydrogels for 10 to 20% GQDs concentration (Fig. 3b-c). Increasing the concentration of GQDs up to 30%, changed the surface of the nanocomposite films that is probably relative to the aggregation phenomenon. The good dispersion of the nanoparticles in the low concentration of GQDs on the surface of CMC matrix is most probably due to the H-bonding interaction between the carboxylate groups in the networks of CMC hydrogels with hydroxyl and carboxylate groups in the GQDs nanoparticle structures. The aggregation phenomenon probably can be related to the friendship nature of GQDs nanoparticles at a high concentration in the polymeric matrix [49].

Fig. 4 shows TEM image of CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel film sample. As seen in Fig. 4, the low density particle aggregation with normal cluster size > 50 nm are visible, which confirmed the well dispersion of GQDs nanoparticles within nanocomposite hydrogel network with lower agglomeration [38].

The pure CMC exhibited a tensile strength of 50.4 MPa (Fig. 5a). Incorporation of GQD with 20% content of the CMC matrix slightly increased the tensile strength that is due to the well dispersion GQDs nanoparticle among the polymeric matrix. The results showed the CMC/GQD nanocomposite has a sufficient strength to distribute the applied force. By increasing the GQDs concentration to 30% the nanocomposite strength was decreased, that is probably due to the aggregation of the GQDs nanoparticle in the high concentration of GQD [21]. The flexibility of this material was determined using the elongation at break values. Pure CMC and CMC/GQDs showed a slight variation in the elongation at break point (Fig. 5b). From the obtained data it can be understood that CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films have suitable flexibility. Due to the flexible nature of prepared CMC/GQDs composite films, these CMC/GQDs nanocomposite films can be potential for the using on any kind of tissue.

Fig. 6a–b shows the swelling ratio of the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films. As it is evident from the graphs the swelling ratio was dramatically increased with time. The swelling ratio of CMC/GQD nanocomposite is lower than pure CMC hydrogel film. In the high concentration of GQD swelling ratio slightly decreased with increasing the GQD nanoparticle concentration. Probably decreasing the swelling capacity of the CMC/GQD in comparison with pure CMC hydrogel is related to the polymeric network rigidity. Because the GQD nanoparticles have the polar group on the surface and edge, during the crosslinking process these nanoparticles can be more joined to the polymer chains thus rigidity of polymer matrix is increased and consequently swelling decrease. As well as, time dependent swelling can be seen in the pure CMC and CMC/GQD nanocomposite which was increased by passing of time.

Biodegradation data discovered the biodegradability of CMC/GQD nanocomposite (Fig. 6c-d). All the hydrogel nanocomposite films have weight loss about 45%, which was increased by increasing GQDs concentration. Also, part of this weight loss can be related to the release glycerol and nanoparticle among the hydrogel network. Thus in the physiological condition, the prepared hydrogel nanocomposite films could have the biodegradation nature [50].

Fig. 7 show the effect of GQDs content on the WVP of the CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogel films. According to the permeability data, by increasing the GQDs concentration in the polymeric matrix the remarkable increase was observed in the WVP values. Probably increasing the permeability of nanocomposite films is depended on the concentration of GQDs nanoparticles that is due to the creation of permeable polymer zone around the GQDs nanoparticles [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD-dependent in vitro drug release and load
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8a and b shows the cumulative release profiles of DOX from the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films achieved at different GQD concentration and pH. In earlier times (10 h) can be seen the fastest rate of drug release for all of the both pH values. After that, the release rate reduces for both pH values. The release percentage of DOX decreases with increasing GQD concentration. To ensure of an inverse relationship between GQD content and drug release performances, two various pH values was studied for the in vitro release of the DOX at different GQD concentration. As can be understood from Fig. 8 the drug release profiles for all tree pHs have been depended on GQD concentration. The release percentage of DOX from the CMC/GQD nanocomposite decreases with increasing GQD content even with changing the pHs.

As can be seen in Fig. 9a capacity of the drug loading in the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogels is depended on the GQD concentration. The drug loading capacity of the nanocomposite was increased by increasing the GQD concentration. From the above evaluation, the interaction between the drug and nanoparticle play an important role in the load and release process, hence dependence on the GQD concentration was observed. In seems that the Π-Π stacking between the drug and nanoparticle to be more important than electrostatic attractions between CMC/hydrogel and drugs because, with changes in the physiological conditions of release, a similar effect can be seen in release percentage with a slight difference. Fig. 8b can be shown the possible interaction between the DOX with GQDs nanoparticle [16]. The morphology of nanocomposite hydrogel films after loading of DOX were shown in the typical SEM images (Fig. 9c-d). As seen, there is only a little difference between morphology of drug-loaded films and blank films.

Fig. 10a illustrates the cumulative release profiles of DOX from the CMC/GQD 30% at different pH values (4.5 and 7.4). The pH sensitivity of in vitro drug release of CMC/GQD was enhanced by increasing the GQD concentration. CMC/GQD 30% was showed an improved pH sensitivity drug release compared to a low concentration of GQD in the CMC/GQD nanocomposite.

From the above evaluation, it was determined that the interactions between the carrier and the drug play an important role in the pH-dependent release of the loaded drug. For DOX an electrostatic attraction among the CMC hydrogel and drug is remarkable; due to the pKa value of the –NH2 groups on the DOX molecules which is 8.2, the –NH2 is highly portioned at pH 7.4 and DOX has a positive charge [52], in contrast, the CMC hydrogel has a positive charge at pH 7.4, so the result was showed the electrostatic attraction is more probable at pH 7.4. Also, the results showed that there is only a little difference between the amounts of pH 7.4 and pH 7 release. A similar interaction can be seen at pH 4.5 but with low power, due to the CMC hydrogel is protonated. The –CO2H functional groups on the CMC chains was protonated partially at pH 4.5 due to the CMC hydrogel has carboxylic groups with pKa 4.6 [53]. Therefore, the release percentage at pH 7.4 should be <4.5 due to the low power interaction, while it was not observed. It seems that for DOX releasing the Π-Π stacking between the drug and GQDs is very preferred respect to the electrostatic attractions, hence low release percentage can be seen at pH 4.5. As well as the swelling rate at pH 4.5 was lower than at pH 7.4 which is ceased to low drug release percentage. Since many pathological processes in the tumor tissues such as endosomes and intracellular lysosomes are most affected local pH change compared with these process in the normal tissues, the pH-sensitive drug delivery performance is very important for the sagacious and smart drug carrier [38].

Fig. 10b demonstrates the compassion of DOX releasing profiles from the pure CMC hydrogel and CMC/GQD hydrogel nanocomposite films with different GQDs concentration. As can be seen, the cumulative release amount of DOX increased by increasing GQD concentrations in the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogels. Whereas the amount of DOX release was increased by increasing GQD concentration the release percentage of the drug was decreased that is probably can be relative to unique interaction among the GQD nanoparticle and drug. According to the above results, whereas DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite showed lower drug release in acidic pH but it could deliver DOX in a controllable and sustained dosage.

Cytotoxicity of the CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films were calculated by using the MTT assay. For the control group, a free blank solution was employed. As seen in Fig. 11a, after incubation for 72 h the blank nanocomposite demonstrated non-significant cytotoxicity to K562 cell and the cell viability remained above 100%. The GQDs nanoparticles were confidently useful to improve the biocompatibility of CMC/GQD nanocomposite. So, for anticancer drug delivery system, CMC/GQD could prove to be a safe carrier. However, as it is seen from the Fig. 11b, due to the high concentration of the active drugs, lead to higher cytotoxicity, so the higher dose of both the DOX solution and the DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite exhibited a dose-dependent manner for cytotoxicity to K562 cell. The cytotoxicity of the free DOX solution was higher than that of DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite at the equivalent DOX concentration and the similar results were reported by previous studies [38,54]. That is due to the strong π-π stacking interaction between the loaded DOX and GQD that resulted in slow and continues the release of the drug from DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite which was lead to in a low cytotoxicity. Since DOX is a non-specificity drug and also it is difficult to sustain the equilibrium between maximum safe concentration and minimum effective concentration, so it's possible to observe severe adverse effects. So a slower drug release rate leads to minimize drug loss and side effect. According to the above results, whereas DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite showed lower cancer cytotoxicity than free DOX solution but it could deliver DOX in a controllable and sustained method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological study of the apoptotic cells
ce:section.ce:para: Round form K562 cells (control) were found under a phase contrast microscopy, while the treated cells were condensed and their adhesiveness to the culture plate was reduced (Fig. 12a). The cell lines were stained with Hoechst 33258 to detect the apoptotic cells (Fig. 12b). The cells were respectively exposed to 6.1, 5.7, 5.4, and 5.1 μg/mL of DOX/CMC, DOX/CMC/GQD 10%, DOX/CMC/GQD 20% and DOX/CMC/GQD 30% (at IC50 values) for 72 h incubated. Large and round nuclei in the fluorescence microscope were observed without fragmentation or condensation in the control cells. However, the treated cells with DOX-loaded nanocomposite, illustrated chromatin fragmentation and condensation of a usual morphological features of apoptosis. The data showed that the DOX-loaded nanocomposite induced apoptosis in the K562 cell lines (Fig. 12b). Comparison of these results with previously published papers in which K562 cells have been used for cytotoxicity evaluation of materials, the introduced method, DOX loaded CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films, showed a novel approach for the apoptotic cells which additionally as a suggestion can be implanted inside skin because of film forming manner.

A flexible nanocomposite hydrogel films with anticancer drug release property were prepared through casting method of GQDs as a nanoparticle and CMC hydrogel as a polymeric matrix. Swelling, degradation, permeability and mechanical tests results showed that CMC/GQDs nanocomposite hydrogel films have a great improvement in swelling, degradation, permeability and mechanical properties with incorporation the GQD nanoparticle. DOX loading and release showed that the incorporation of GQD into CMC films caused a pH-sensitivity and consecutive prolonged the release of DOX. Cytotoxicity results exhibited that the prepared DOX/CMC/GQD nanocomposite could be possessed the great potential as a long-lasting and high-efficiency anticancer agent and CMC/GQD nanocomposite showed non-significant toxicity against blood cancer cells (K562).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 938645702) and RCPN of the Tabriz University of Medical Science for the financial supports for this research.
